system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — modern-jazz-guitar-concepts

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

The book teaches jazz guitar improvisation as a layered system of harmonic substitution, melodic construction, and technical vocabulary, building from foundational diatonic principles outward through increasingly chromatic and symmetrical territories. Its pedagogical arc is cumulative: Chapter 1 establishes the core logic of shared-tone arpeggio selection over diatonic seventh chords, Chapters 2 and 3 introduce chromatic coloring and harmonic minor applications, Chapters 4 and 5 extend that logic into altered and diminished scale territories, and Chapter 6 synthesizes everything into a complete solo analysis. Each chapter adds a new layer of harmonic material while reinforcing the same underlying principle: melodic notes must be justified by their relationship to chord tones, and technique serves harmony, not the other way around.

## Harmonic Rationale as the Central Principle

The book's master principle, stated first in Chapter 1 and never abandoned, is that arpeggio and scale selection should be grounded in shared tones with the underlying chord rather than in root movement or habit. Chapter 1 demonstrates this through the m7b5-on-the-3rd substitution over dominant seventh chords, establishes a hierarchy of substitution options (3rd-rooted being most versatile, 5th- and 7th-rooted requiring caution), and reframes familiar scales—D Minor Pentatonic becomes Dm7add11—to make their harmonic rationale explicit. This same logic governs Chapter 4's altered scale instruction, where the master selects the G#m7b5 arpeggio over Bb7alt precisely because it preserves the guide tones (3rd and 7th: F and Ab) while highlighting the b9 and b13 extensions. Chapter 5 extends the principle further by isolating the four major triads embedded in the half-whole diminished scale and teaching them as upper-structure voicings that express the 13b9 dominant color. Throughout, the book insists that the student understand *why* a choice works before drilling it.

## Characteristic Systems and Terminology

The book builds a coherent technical lexicon that recurs across chapters. **Shell voicings** (root, 3rd, 7th only) appear in Chapters 1 and 4 as the harmonic skeleton beneath melodic elaboration. **Guide tones** anchor arpeggio selection in Chapters 4 and 5. **Quartal and quintal harmony**—chords and arpeggios stacked in fourths and fifths—appear in Chapters 1, 5, and 6 as an alternative to tertian thinking. **Chromatic enclosures** receive a full chapter (Chapter 2) and return in Chapters 3, 4, and 6; the two-note and four-note enclosure constructions are load-bearing throughout. The **bebop melodic counterpoint rule**—large intervals resolve stepwise in the opposite direction—is introduced in Chapter 1, restated in Chapter 3, and functions as a persistent constraint on line construction. The **Coltrane 1-2-3-5 pattern** (forward, reverse, and minor variant 1-b3-4-5) appears in Chapter 1 as the primary device for breaking scalic lines over fast changes.

## Rules and Prescriptions

The book is unusually explicit about rules. Chromatic passing notes belong on off-beats in hardbop and bebop (Chapter 2); this is stated as a foundational constraint, not a suggestion. The C note over G7 is avoided because it creates an unwanted sus4 tension (Chapter 1). Enclosures must remain anchored to chord and scale tones to avoid becoming melodic noise (Chapter 2). The legato technique—hammer-ons, pull-offs, and slides on the same string—is prescribed specifically to keep chromatic passing notes soft while scale tones are accented (Chapter 2). Enclosure resolution on beat 4 following a beat 2 placement produces a modern, unresolved Metheny-esque quality, which the book frames as an advanced option rather than a default (Chapter 2). The half-whole diminished scale's symmetrical minor-third structure is explicitly framed as a learning-load reducer: the student need only learn one triad shape and rotate it to access all four embedded major triads (Chapter 5).

## Melodic Construction Method

The book teaches a concrete method for constructing jazz lines. The ascending-arpeggio-plus-descending-scale formula, introduced in Chapter 1, functions as the master's signature melodic shape. Over any chord, the process is: identify the chord's guide tones, select an arpeggio whose notes overlap the chord's extensions, approach target notes via enclosure (diatonic above, chromatic below, or their inversions), and resolve large intervallic leaps stepwise in the opposite direction. Chapter 3 adds a motif-based versus target-note distinction: the student can organize a line either by developing a recurring rhythmic or intervallic cell, or by working backward from a specific chord tone to land. Chapter 4 introduces rhythmic variation—eighth-note triplets and sixteenth-note subdivisions—as a way to embellish arpeggios and imply unwritten passing chords. Chapter 5 teaches triad cycling through neck-crossing positional exercises rather than scalar runs, specifically to build melodic range and fretboard navigation simultaneously. Chapter 6 closes the method loop by applying all of the above to a full solo, analyzed bar by bar, with the explicit instruction that multiple valid interpretations of any phrase are acceptable—the goal is internalization of the system, not memorization of a single reading.

## Synthesis and Scope

Chapter 6 makes explicit what the earlier chapters imply: the book's endpoint is a guitarist who can move fluidly between diatonic substitution, chromatic enclosure, harmonic minor coloring, altered dominant tension, and diminished symmetry within a single improvised line, selecting tools by ear and harmonic context. The pedagogical signature across all six chapters is the same: isolate a small vocabulary item (one arpeggio type, one enclosure construction, one triad set), establish its harmonic rationale in shared tones, apply it within a II-V-I or similar cadential context, and then connect it explicitly to the broader system. Voice-leading—whether by common tone, contrary motion, or encirclement—is the connective tissue that makes the system coherent, and it is the criterion by which the book asks students to evaluate every melodic choice they make.

# Chapter 1 — Chapter One — No Alterations or Passing Notes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter One teaches diatonic seventh chord substitution and upper-structure arpeggio selection by shared tones rather than root movement, establishing the book’s central principle through progressively complex rules: the arpeggio-on-the-3rd substitution (m7b5 on dominant sevenths) emerges as the most versatile technique, while 5th- and 7th-rooted options require more caution. The master’s characteristic moves visible here are the ascending-arpeggio-plus-descending-scale melodic formula and the Coltrane 1-2-3-5 pattern (applied forward, reverse, and in the minor variant 1-b3-4-5), both used to break up scalic lines and create interest over fast-tempo chord changes. The chapter introduces load-bearing terminology including shell voicings (root, 3rd, 7th only), quartal harmony (4th-based stacking), and the reframing of common scales—like D Minor Pentatonic—as extended arpeggios (Dm7add11), grounding every technique in harmonic rationale rather than mere fingering habit. A counterpoint principle (large intervals resolve stepwise in opposite direction) and a specific avoidance rule (C note over G7 creates unwanted sus4 tension) round out the chapter’s ruleset, which culminates in a consolidated II-V-I practice assignment that chains all techniques together.

# Chapter 2 — Chapter Two — Chromatic Enclosures

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter Two teaches chromatic enclosures—short phrases mixing scale notes and chromatic passing notes to emphasize target notes—grounded in the foundational rule that chromatic notes belong on off-beats in hardbop and bebop jazz. The master’s characteristic approach emphasizes legato technique with hammer-ons, pull-offs, and slides to accent passing notes softly while resolving to scale notes, keeping both on the same string where possible. Two-note and four-note enclosure constructions anchor the technique: two-note enclosures place a chromatic note and diatonic note around a triad tone in either order, while four-note enclosures align resolution with strong beats (1 and 3) and function either as suspended sounds or as tension-and-pull devices toward chord tones. Key harmonic moves include playing arpeggios from the 5th degree (with added 3rd) over II chords and arpeggios from the 3rd degree as a bebop device, with diatonic notes substituting when chromatic gaps don’t exist. Advanced placement—such as resolving enclosures on weak beats like beat 4 (following a beat 2 placement)—creates modern, unresolved melodic qualities reminiscent of Metheny, but the chapter closes with a cautionary principle: enclosures must remain anchored to chord and scale tones to avoid overuse and maintain their gravitational function.

# Chapter 3 — Chapter Three — Harmonic Minor on the V

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

This chapter teaches how to construct melodic lines over harmonic minor changes, particularly on the V chord, using diatonic triads, arpeggios, and bebop counterpoint rules as primary tools. The author demonstrates characteristic moves including the #9 as a chromatic passing note over dominant chords, enclosure formulas (diatonic above, chromatic below), and the technique of targeting the V chord’s third by arpeggiating from the II chord’s fifth. Key terminology introduced includes the bebop melodic counterpoint rule (resolving large intervals stepwise in the opposite direction), motif-based versus target-note approaches to chord navigation, triad pairs with no common notes, and the construction of non-diatonic arpeggios like Bbm7b5 and C7#5 by reordering scale tones from alternate roots. The chapter emphasizes varying melodic ideas through different triad patterns (1-3-5 sequences, quartal arpeggios) and contextual chord-tone omission, showing that ambiguous voicings remain effective when heard within the harmonic framework of the key.

# Chapter 4 — Chapter Four — Altered Scale

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

The altered scale, the seventh mode of the melodic minor scale, creates an "outside" sound over dominant seventh chords and is typically understood through its parent melodic minor relationship—for example, Bb Altered as the seventh mode of B Melodic Minor. The master technique centers on guide tones: the 3rd and 7th of the altered dominant (F and Ab in Bb7alt) are preserved by selecting arpeggios like G#m7b5, which highlights the b9, b13, and 3rd extensions while functioning as a tritone substitute relationship. Shell voicings reduce chords to root, 3rd, and 7th, and triplet rhythms on seventh arpeggios deliver bebop phrasing; chromatic passing notes and rhythmic variation—eighth-note triplets, sixteenth-note subdivisions—embellish arpeggios and suggest unwritten passing chords for forward momentum. Key moves include resolving augmented triads (a bebop trademark), using minor pentatonic from the 3rd over tonic major chords, and deploying four-note chromatic enclosures to suspend and delay arrival on target chord tones. Load-bearing terms include altered dominant, guide tones, tritone substitute, shell voicing, chromatic passing notes, and chromatic enclosure.

# Chapter 5 — Chapter Five — Diminished Scale on the Dom7th Chord

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter Five teaches the half-whole diminished scale—an eight-note symmetrical construction built from two interlocking diminished 7th chords a semitone apart—and its primary application as upper-structure triads over dominant 7th chords, particularly the idiomatic 13b9 voicing. The master’s characteristic pedagogical move is isolating the four major triads contained within the scale (F, Ab, B, D) and teaching them through positional cycling and neck-crossing exercises rather than scalar runs, leveraging the scale’s inherent minor 3rd symmetry to reduce learning load. Load-bearing terms introduced include the half-whole diminished scale formula (1 b9 #9 3 b5 5 6 b7), upper-structure triad, the 13b9 dominant extension, stacking triads for melodic range, and the connection to the altered scale through the B major triad’s shared intervals. The chapter emphasizes voice-leading transitions between triads—particularly how Ab and D major triads together convey F7 tensions of #9, b9, and 13—and frames triad exercises as tests of melodic logic and neck navigation rather than mechanistic patterns.

# Chapter 6 — Chapter Six — Putting It All Together

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter Six synthesizes the melodic techniques developed throughout the book by demonstrating how to construct sophisticated solo lines using triad pairs, arpeggios, and scale embellishments over jazz chord changes. The master’s characteristic moves visible here include voice-leading triads by common notes and encirclement, combining non-diatonic triad pairs (like B and Ab major over F7) to generate specific harmonic extensions, and deploying signature inversion-skip patterns attributed to canonical guitarists like George Benson and Grant Green. Load-bearing terminology introduced includes quintal arpeggios (stacks of fifths), encirclement as a voice-leading device, the harmonic minor as a dominant pull toward the next chord, and the principle of repeated melodic statements with slight alteration to fit new harmonies. The chapter anchors learning through detailed bar-by-bar analysis of a complete solo, explicitly inviting students to develop multiple valid interpretations of the same phrase rather than memorizing a single reading. Practice assignments emphasize triad pair improvisation and experimentation with open voicings to internalize fretboard connections across the instrument.

# Chapter 1 — Chapter One — No Alterations or Passing Notes

_Pages 14-28._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Diatonic 7th chord substitution

> Take, for example, an Fmaj7 chord. When improvising over this chord you
> can use an Fmaj7 arpeggio. But you can also look for arpeggios that have
> notes in common with Fmaj7. The most common candidate is the one found
> on the 3rd (A) — Am7. Fmaj7 and Am7 have three notes in common. The
> Am7 arpeggio contains the same notes as Fmaj7 apart from the root, F.
> Mixing these arpeggios when improvising gives you more options for
> creating musical colours.

**Significance (p. 14).** Establishes the book's central substitution principle — choosing arpeggios by shared tones rather than by the root chord — which underpins every lick example that follows.

## Upper-structure arpeggios over G7

> Jazz musicians will often play a new arpeggio from any note in the
> underlying chord. For example, if the underlying chord is G7 (which contains
> the notes G, B, D and F) we will play arpeggios from the third (B) fifth (D) or
> even the seventh (F).

**Significance (p. 14).** The key rule governing all upper-structure arpeggio choices throughout the chapter, defining when and why a musician selects a substitute arpeggio.

## Arpeggio-on-the-3rd rule

> As you can see, playing a m7b5 chord on the 3rd of a dominant 7 chord
> causes us to avoid playing the root (G) and adds the rich-sounding 9th (A).
> Playing an arpeggio on the 3rd of the chord is one of the most common uses
> of arpeggios in jazz. It sounds so good that many musicians always use it
> instead of the original arpeggio.

**Significance (p. 15).** Codifies the single most important substitution rule in the chapter — m7b5 on the 3rd of a dominant — and explicitly elevates it above other options.

## Higher-chord-tone arpeggio caution

> As you move up the chord and play arpeggios from the higher notes (5th and
> 7th), you include fewer notes from the original chord, so you need to be a bit
> more careful.

**Significance (p. 15).** A direct practice rule warning that 5th- and 7th-rooted substitutions carry more harmonic risk, calibrating how boldly the technique should be applied.

## Ascending arpeggio + descending scale lick

> This lick also illustrates a common concept where a melody is created with
> an ascending arpeggio and a descending scale run.

**Significance (p. 16).** Names a structural melodic formula — ascend by arpeggio, descend by scale — that recurs across multiple examples and is one of the method's characteristic moves.

## Coltrane 1-2-3-5 pattern

> Coltrane mastered the approach of using four-note groupings to spell out
> chords as a way of navigating bars that contain two chords, played at a fast
> tempo. He used a 1 2 3 5 pattern (the 1st, 2nd, 3rd and 5th degrees of the
> scale) and varied the order of the sequence to create different melodies. Here
> I’m playing it in reverse over the G7 chord: 5 (D), 3 (B), 2 (A) and 1 (G).
> Coltrane patterns are a useful way to break up scalic lines and create interest
> while playing diatonically.

**Significance (p. 17).** Defines the Coltrane pattern concept with its exact interval formula and demonstrates both forward and reverse application, making it actionable.

## D Minor Pentatonic as Dm7add11

> Example 1h uses the D Minor Pentatonic scale in bar one. This may seem an
> obvious choice to play over Dm7, but it can be used very effectively. Imagine
> the notes of D Minor Pentatonic as though they are an arpeggio and they spell
> a Dm7add11 chord:
>
> D F G A C
> 1 3 1l(or4) |5 7

**Significance (p. 18).** Reframes pentatonic notes as an extended arpeggio voicing, giving a harmonic rationale for a common choice.

## Shell voicing definition

> A shell voicing is a chord voicing that use only the root, 3rd and 7th intervals.
> Jazz musicians often use just two notes to define the sound of a chord, such
> as root and 3rd, root and 7th, or 3rd and 7th without the root. Playing lines
> like this with no exotic ingredients forces us to dig deeper to create a strong
> melody.

**Significance (p. 19).** Defines shell voicings precisely and gives the pedagogical reason for using them in lines.

## Quartal harmony definition

> Traditionally, chords are formed by stacking intervals of a 3rd. For example,
> the notes C, E, G and B form Cmaj7. But it is also possible to stack chords in
> Ath intervals e.g. C, F, B and E. Stacking notes in 4ths is known
> as quartal harmony and was popularised in jazz by Miles Davis during his
> modal period.

**Significance (p. 19).** The chapter's definition of quartal harmony, establishing the concept before it appears as both a melodic and voicing device.

## Counterpoint rule: large intervals resolve stepwise

> This lick also demonstrates a common rule in
> counterpoint melody writing: to resolve a series of large ascending intervals
> with a stepwise movement in the opposite direction.

**Significance (p. 21).** Introduces a classical counterpoint principle as an explicit compositional rule for jazz melody construction.

## G7sus4 tension via C note

> Practise using Dm7 over G7 by playing a static chord and superimposing the
> arpeggios listed in the table above. The only thing to watch for here is to
> choose carefully when to use the C note. Playing a C over a G7 chord will
> suggest a G7sus4 sound. This might be desirable in some instances but not in
> others.

**Significance (p. 23).** A specific avoidance/preference rule about when the 11th creates sus4 ambiguity over a dominant chord.

## Chapter assignments: II V I lick writing

> Write a set of five or more licks, or a short solo, over the II V I progression
> using:
>
> Triads or arpeggios chained together as shown in Example le
> Diatonic triads or arpeggios in sequences
>
> Triad inversions
>
> Using the arpeggio on the 3rd or 5th of the chord
>
> The pentatonic scale from the root of a II or VI minor chord, or the 3rd
> of the I chord (e.g., E Minor Pentatonic over Cmaj7
>
> Quartal shapes, chord voicings with added notes, or shell voicings
>
> Coltrane patterns (1 2 35 on a major chord, 1 b3 4.5 on a minor chord)

**Significance (p. 24).** Consolidates every technique taught in the chapter into a single prioritized practice list, including the Coltrane minor-chord variant (1 b3 4 5).

# Chapter 2 — Chapter Two — Chromatic Enclosures

_Pages 29-42._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## chromatic notes on off-beats

> we can play 1/8th note
> jazz lines that begin on a scale note and cause all the strong chord tones to
> fall on the beat, and the chromatic passing notes on the off-beat. This is
> useful, because in hardbop / bebop jazz, chromatic notes are generally placed
> on the off-beats.

**Significance (p. 29).** Foundational rule of the chapter: chromatic passing notes belong on off-beats.

## legato technique for chromaticism

> I tend to play these exercises using a legato technique with lots of hammer-
> ons, pull-offs and slides. Aim to pick a chromatic note and resolve it to the
> following note with a legato movement. This should result in nice phrasing,
> because the passing note is accented and the scale note is softer. To aid this
> approach, I try to keep passing notes and scale notes on the same string where
> possible.

**Significance (p. 29).** Primary technique instruction for executing chromatic lines, with both mechanics and phrasing rationale.

## chromatic enclosure definition

> A chromatic enclosure is a short phrase that mixes scale notes and chromatic
> passing notes to emphasise a target note. Think of it in these terms: you want
> to highlight one particular note, but surround it with other notes that lead to
> it. In jazz, enclosures can range in length anything from two to ten notes.

**Significance (p. 31).** Chapter's core concept definition.

## two-note enclosure construction

> The concept is, for each note
> of a C major triad, to play a chromatic note below and a diatonic note above.
> Example 2e shows how this sounds for a C major triad in the 8th position.
>
> The first two bars of this lick place the chromatic note first, followed by the
> diatonic note, then the triad note. The second two bars flip this sequence
> around — the diatonic note is played first, then the chromatic note, then the
> triad note. Either way sounds good, so you can find your preference.

**Significance (p. 32).** Defines the exact construction rule for two-note enclosures with both orderings as valid options.

## four-note enclosure function

> We can take this idea a step further with four-note enclosures. These are very
> common in modern jazz, since they provide an easy way to emphasise the
> basic jazz groove, in which the “heavy” beats fall on the 1 and the 3. With
> more notes to play with, there can be more permutations of the sequence.

**Significance (p. 33).** Explains why four-note enclosures are used — to align resolution with strong beats 1 and 3.

## arpeggio from 5th degree over II chord

> Playing the arpeggio from the 5th degree of the minor seventh chord creates a
> nice sound over a II chord, but you could consider adding the 3rd of the Dm7
> chord, since that note is not in the arpeggio. Adding the third helps to clearly
> define the sound of the underlying chord.

**Significance (p. 35).** Reusable harmonic rule for generating characteristic bebop lines over II chords.

## arpeggio from 3rd degree (bebop device)

> Playing an arpeggio from the 3rd of a chord is a useful device to produce
> characteristic bebop lines.

**Significance (p. 36).** Names and endorses a core bebop substitution principle.

## diatonic note substitution for chromatic gap

> Since it’s
> not possible to add a chromatic note in between, I added a G note (the
> diatonic note above F). Listen to how natural that approach sounds.

**Significance (p. 37).** Practical rule for handling cases where a half-step gap doesn't exist.

## four-note enclosure: two functions

> Four-note enclosures can work in two ways. They can precede a chord tone
> to create a suspended sound, or they can be used as a way of “pulling”
> towards the chord tone you intend to resolve to.

**Significance (p. 39).** Conceptual taxonomy of four-note enclosure usage: suspension vs. tension-and-pull.

## off-beat enclosure placement (Metheny)

> The chromatic enclosure in this bar is
> placed on beat 2, so that it will resolve on beat 4 of the bar. This leaves a
> chromatic note on beat 3, which is a strong beat in jazz. The effect is to give
> the line an unresolved quality, again reminiscent of Metheny. Using this idea
> throughout a solo can open things up, because the melodic lines will sound
> less connected to the underlying chords, achieving a more modern sound.

**Significance (p. 41).** Advanced rhythmic displacement rule — placing an enclosure on beat 2 to resolve on beat 4.

## enclosures must target chord/scale tones

> Chromatic enclosures and passing notes are a big part of the sound we
> associate with the bebop and hardbop melodic language. However, be careful
> to exercise some control over how you use them: remain aware that their
> main purpose is to target or gravitate towards scale / chord tones.

**Significance (p. 42).** Closing rule warning against overuse and anchoring the technique to resolution.

# Chapter 3 — Chapter Three — Harmonic Minor on the V

_Pages 43-52._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## diatonic triads as melodic material

> Example 3b demonstrates the diatonic triads of F Harmonic
> Minor, written as a scale exercise. Playing through this will be of immense
> help in creating interesting melodic ideas, so it doesn’t sound as though
> you’re simply running up and down scales. Solos that only contain 1/8th
> scalic ideas get boring pretty quickly!

**Significance (p. 43).** Establishes the core pedagogical move: converting scale knowledge into melodic vocabulary via triads.

## recommended arpeggios over C7

> the ones I have selected
> below will convey a C7 (b9, b13) sound. The arpeggios I recommend are:
>
> © Cmaj
>
> e Edim
>
> * Gdim
>
> e Abaug
>
> e Bbdim
>
> ° C7
>
> e Edim
>
> e Abmaj7#5

**Significance (p. 44).** Author's explicit selection of arpeggios that best express the harmonic minor sound over a dominant V chord.

## bebop melodic counterpoint rule

> One of the melodic counterpoint rules commonly used in bebop is that a large
> interval in one direction is best resolved with a stepwise motion in the
> opposite direction.

**Significance (p. 46).** Named compositional rule that recurs across multiple example lines.

## #9 as chromatic passing note over V7

> The dominant 7th line in this example uses a common “trick” — which is to
> add in the #9 as well as the b9. In this instance the #9 is an Eb, which sounds
> great over the C7 chord. It functions a bit like a chromatic passing note, as it
> leads nicely to E — the 3rd of C7.

**Significance (p. 47).** Defines a reusable dominant-line device — combining b9 and #9 — and explains voice-leading logic.

## enclosure with diatonic note above, chromatic below

> the lick played over the Gm7 encloses the chord tone
> with a diatonic note above and a chromatic note below. The same idea is
> applied first to the root, then the 3rd.

**Significance (p. 47).** Specific enclosure formula applicable to any chord tone.

## motif-based vs target-note approach

> Notice that the transition from Gm7 to C7 is accomplished with a motif. The
> descending Bbmaj7 arpeggio motif is repeated using the descending E
> diminished arpeggio. This is a different approach to moving from one chord
> to the next by targeting chord tones. It’s good to have both motif-based and
> target note ideas in your arsenal of lines.

**Significance (p. 48).** Two distinct compositional strategies for navigating chord changes.

## 5th of II chord targets 3rd of V

> Playing an arpeggio from the 5th of the II chord is a fantastic way to target
> the third of the V chord that follows it. To the ears, it sounds like a suspended
> chord, but without moving very far from the key.

**Significance (p. 49).** Transferable rule for II–V voice-leading.

## triads in 1-3-5 sequence for variation

> A useful concept to bring more variation to your melodic lines is to use triads
> arranged in different patterns. Example 3k begins with a G minor triad played
> in a3 135 sequence. It continues into a quartal arpeggio leading from a G
> note, using the same pattern. The effect is to create a repeating motif.

**Significance (p. 49).** Reordering triad tones and quartal arpeggios as a motivic technique.

## Bbm7b5 constructed from harmonic minor

> Strictly speaking,
> Bbm7b5 is not a diatonic chord of F Harmonic Minor, but it is possible to
> construct this arpeggio with notes from the scale. Below this is illustrated by
> showing the F Harmonic Minor scale played from Bb.
>
> F Harmonic Minor| Bb| C Db E F G Ab
> (from Bb)
>
> Bbm7 Bb Db F Ab
> Bbm7b5 Bb Db E Ab
>
> Using Bbm7b5 over the C7 chord imposes the intervals of the b7, b9, 3 and
> #5. They sound great!

**Significance (p. 50).** Demonstrates deriving non-diatonic arpeggios by restarting scale tones from a different root.

## chromatic approach to II chord third

> Over a II chord, a common bebop device is to approach the third
> chromatically from a whole step above. In the example below this is the
> chromatic descent from C to Bb.

**Significance (p. 50).** Classic bebop approach-note device for the II chord with concrete pitches.

## triad pairs with no common notes

> Pairing triads like this creates a very strong melodic
>
>
> structure, and is a familiar sound to our ears. Modern jazz saxophonists such
> as George Garzone and Michael Brecker often solo using pairs of triads that
> have no notes in common, which creates an angular, unresolved sound.

**Significance (p. 51).** Contextualizes triad pairs within a named modern jazz tradition.

## omitting chord tone for open sound

> This time I have omitted the 3rd (Bb) of
> Gm7 which immediately makes the line more open sounding. In isolation, it
>
> 8
> 5 7 5 5
> 7 8 8 6
>
>
>
> could sound like a G7sus4 chord, but is fine when we hear it in the context of
> our key of F Major. We can play challenging lines like this as long as we are
> conscious of their overall effect on the music.

**Significance (p. 52).** Articulates a rule about contextual tolerance for ambiguous lines via chord-tone omission.

## C7#5 arpeggio construction

> The latter is another useful arpeggio that is not diatonic to F
> Harmonic Minor, but can be constructed from a C7 triad with an added
> augmented Sth.

**Significance (p. 52).** Defines C7#5 as a non-diatonic but scale-compatible option.

# Chapter 4 — Chapter Four — Altered Scale

_Pages 53-62._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Altered scale definition

> The altered scale is the seventh mode of the melodic minor scale and one of
> the most commonly used when soloing over dominant chords. Typically used
> over an altered dominant chord, it creates an “outside” sound which is then
> resolved when the progression moves to the I chord.

**Significance (p. 53).** Foundational definition of the altered scale.

## Bb Altered / B Melodic Minor relationship

> The Bb Altered scale is the seventh mode of B Melodic Minor, so let’s
> examine the triads and arpeggios we can use in terms of B Minor, for the
> sake of simplicity.

**Significance (p. 53).** Establishes the reframing technique.

## Identifying guide tones of altered dominant

> The defining tones of a Bb7alt chord are the 3rd and 7th (F and Ab), so if we
> use arpeggios from the Bb Altered scale that contain those intervals we can
>
> stay close to the dominant sound and introduce some cool altered extensions.
> Only two chords contain both notes: E7 and G#m7b5. (G# = Ab)

**Significance (p. 54).** Rule for selecting arpeggios over an altered dominant via guide tones.

## E7 as tritone substitute

> E7 is the tritone substitute of Bb7.

**Significance (p. 54).** Names the harmonic relationship justifying E7 over Bb7alt.

## G#m7b5 as best altered dominant arpeggio

> G#m7b5 is related to E7, as it can be viewed as an arpeggio built on the 3rd
> of E7. Of the two options, the G#m7b5 arpeggio is probably the best
> candidate to spell out a Bb7alt sound.

**Significance (p. 54).** Practical recommendation.

## G#m7b5 interval map over Bb7alt

> The table below shows which intervals of Bb7alt are highlighted when you
> play a G#m7b5 arpeggio:
>
> Arpeggio G# B D F#
> Note
>
> Relative to b7 b9 3 b13
> Bb

**Significance (p. 54).** Interval mapping showing altered extensions.

## Shell voicing definition

> A shell voicing consists of the root and
> defining 3rd and 7th intervals of a chord. An E7 shell voicing would
> therefore be E, G# and D.

**Significance (p. 55).** Defines shell voicing.

## Triplets on 7th arpeggios bebop trick

> Using triplets to play 7th arpeggios is a great bebop trick that works well in a
> more moder jazz context as well.

**Significance (p. 56).** Rhythmic technique rule.

## Minor pentatonic from the 3rd over tonic major

> Playing the minor
>
>
> pentatonic scale from the 3rd is a great device to use over tonic major chords.

**Significance (p. 57).** Transferable rule for tonic major chords.

## Chromatic passing notes on arpeggios

> The beginning of this next example is a beautiful way of adding chromatic
> notes that connect arpeggio notes. In bar one, the addition of chromatic
> passing notes serves to embellish this Fm9 arpeggio. The use of 1/16th notes
> makes the rhythm more interesting.

**Significance (p. 58).** Chromatic embellishment with rhythmic subdivision.

## Rhythmic variation with triplet passing notes

> Lines can be made more interesting by introducing rhythmic variation. In the
> next example, an 8th note triplet is used for the chromatic passing notes that
> introduce the melody.

**Significance (p. 59).** Rhythmic variation rule.

## Augmented triad resolution (bop trait)

> The resolution of augmented triads is a common bop trait that has carried
> over into more modern styles. In this instance it is achieved by resolving the

**Significance (p. 60).** Augmented triad resolution as stylistic device.

## Suggesting unwritten passing chords

> Suggesting passing chords that aren’t written is a great way to create motion
> towards a chord change. In Example 4j, the line in bar one begins with an
> Fm7 arpeggio line. In bar two there is an Abm7b5 arpeggio played over the
> Bb7alt chord. To connect these two ideas, in the latter half of bar one we
> have a Gm7 arpeggio. The effect is a strong motion from the Fm7 to Bb7alt
> chords.

**Significance (p. 58).** Imply unwritten passing chords for momentum.

## Four-note chromatic enclosure to suspend chord tone

> The final example of this chapter begins with a four-note chromatic enclosure
> (discussed in Chapter Two). In Example 40 the purpose of the enclosure is to
> suspend the 3rd of the Fm7 chord until beat 3, before the line continues with
> an Abmaj7 arpeggio.

**Significance (p. 61).** Delay arrival on target tone via enclosure.

# Chapter 5 — Chapter Five — Diminished Scale on the Dom7th Chord

_Pages 63-73._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Half-Whole Diminished Scale Definition

> The half-whole diminished scale is a fairly recent addition to Western music.
> Though it appeared in earlier works, it didn’t become a common device until
> the late 18th century. It is an eight note scale with a symmetrical, almost
> “mathematical” construction that in many ways sets it apart from tonal music
> and functional harmony.

**Significance (p. 63).** Defines the core subject of the chapter.

## Diminished Scale as Interlocking Dim7 Chords

> The diminished scale can be viewed as two interlocking diminished 7th
> chords that repeat symmetrically in minor 3rd intervals. One of the best ways
> to use a “synthetic” scale like this is to pick out and isolate the triads it
> contains.

**Significance (p. 63).** Conceptual key — triad extraction strategy.

## Constructing the F Half-Whole Diminished Scale

> The Half Whole Diminished scale can be constructed from two diminished
> arpeggios a semitone apart. To construct an F Half Whole Diminished scale,
> you combine F diminished and Gb diminished arpeggios.
>
> F Diminished: F Ab B D
> Gb Diminished: Gb A C Eb
>
> Arranged in order of pitch from F, this gives us the eight notes of the F Half
> Whole Diminished scale: F Gb Ab ABCD EDF
>
> As a formula, the diminished scale is 1 b9 #9 3 b5 5 6 b7

**Significance (p. 64).** Core construction rule and scale formula.

## Minor 3rd Symmetry for Arpeggio Transposition

> Due
> to the nature of its construction using diminished arpeggios, everything that
> exists inside the Half Whole Diminished scale can be moved in minor 3rds,
> so we only have to look at the chords we can build on the first two notes (in
> this case F and Gb) and we can find the rest by transposing them in minor 3rd
> intervals.

**Significance (p. 65).** Symmetry rule reducing learning load to two roots.

## Chords Built from F in the Diminished Scale

> Building chords from the F gives us these chords:
>
> F F A C
> Fm F Ab C
> F(b5) F A B
> Fdim F Ab B
> F7 F A C Eb
> Fm7 F Ab C Eb
> F7(b5) F A B Eb
> Fdim7 F Ab B Eb

**Significance (p. 65).** Exhaustive arpeggio inventory on the first scale degree.

## Scale Degrees Over F7 Root

> F Gb Ab A B C D Eb
> 1 b9 #9 3 #11 13 b7
>
> ul
>
> This scale contains rich extensions such as the b9, #9 and 13th. A dominant
> chord constructed with every extension would be an F13b9#11, although this
> would be a F7b5 if the C (5th) is omitted.

**Significance (p. 66).** Interval-function map over dominant root.

## 13b9 Upper-Structure Triad Concept

> The most common sound associated with this scale is probably the 13b9.
> Guitarists generally think of this as an upper-structure triad placed above a
> dominant 7th chord. E.g. F713b9 is a D major triad over an F7 chord, spelt F
> ACEbDDGDA.

**Significance (p. 66).** Most idiomatic application — upper-structure triad.

## Four Major Triads in the Diminished Scale

> This chapter will focus on using major triads to create the diminished sound.
> The four major triads are F, Ab, B and D. Below are a few exercises to help
> you work on these triads and their inversions, and to help you connect them
> together to create your own lines.

**Significance (p. 67).** Defines narrow actionable focus — four triads.

## Cycling Triads in Position Up the Neck

> Another way to practise these triads is to mix and match the inversions, but
> play in the same position. Example 5h demonstrates cycling through the
> triads in a single position before moving to the next position to cycle through
> them again. All the triads are played on the D, G and B strings.

**Significance (p. 68).** Positional cycling exercise rule.

## Connecting Triads Across the Neck

> Example 5i shows one way to connect the triads together in order to
> improvise across the neck. Exercises like this are a great way to test your
> ability to locate the next triad in the sequence and create a melody that makes
> sense.

**Significance (p. 69).** Pedagogical purpose of connecting exercises.

## Ab and D Triads Conveying F7 Extensions

> The combination of the Ab and D major triads conveys the sound of an F7
> with a #9 (Ab), b9 (Gb) and 13 (D). The melody between the Ab and the D
> creates a voice leading transition between the two, as the Ab moves to A and
> the Eb moves to D.

**Significance (p. 70).** Explains tensions and voice leading between triads.

## Stacking Triads for Colourful Melodies

> The second example demonstrates how “stacking” triads can yield some
> colourful, yet logical-sounding melodies with a fairly big range.

**Significance (p. 71).** Introduces 'stacking' as named technique.

## B Major Triad Intervals Against F Root

> The B major triad is not unique to the diminished sound since it is also
> diatonic to F# Melodic Minor (AKA the F Altered scale). The B major triad
> gives us the #11 (B), b7 (D#) and b9 (F#) intervals against an F root.
> Combined with the D major triad, we also have a 13th and a 3rd.

**Significance (p. 72).** Cross-scale connection between diminished and altered.

# Chapter 6 — Chapter Six — Putting It All Together

_Pages 74-87._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## F major triad colour

> You may
> have noticed that I don’t often use the F major triad because it doesn’t add
> any colour and only so many 1/8th notes are available in a 4/4 bar!

**Significance (p. 74).** Rule about triad selection — omit triads that add no harmonic colour.

## Triad chaining by common notes

> In this example the melody relies on connecting the D and Ab major triads by
> linking the top two notes of the D triad to the lowest two notes of the Ab
> triad.

**Significance (p. 74).** Voice-leading mechanism for D/Ab triad pairs.

## Triad chaining — encirclement

> Example 5n uses a similar device — chaining triads together by using the last
> two notes of one triad to encircle the first note of the next.

**Significance (p. 74).** Defines encirclement as a triad-to-triad voice-leading device.

## Legato for string changes

> The line is executed with legato technique to make is easier for the right hand
> to play the many string changes.

**Significance (p. 75).** Technique instruction linking legato to right-hand string-change ergonomics.

## Arpeggio from 5th — missing 3rd problem

> The problem with using the arpeggio built from the 5th of a minor chord is
> that it does not contain the 3rd, which is an important interval to
> communicate the sound of the chord. A standard way of dealing with this is
> to use the arpeggio to encircle the 3rd

**Significance (p. 75).** Identifies weakness of common arpeggio choice and supplies remedy.

## F7 #11 and b9 with triad pair

> The F7 line uses first inversion F major and B major triads. This combination
> highlights the #11 and b9 extensions.

**Significance (p. 75).** Maps a specific triad pair to extensions over F7.

## Ascending 6th interval — counterpoint rule

> An interval skip of a 6th is a beautiful way to add variation to a scale run.
> The line played over the Cm7 in this example opens with an ascending 6th
> interval from G to Eb. The traditional counterpoint rule for a melody like this
> demands that the tension of the large ascending interval is resolved with step-
> wise motion in a descending direction. This musical “rule” doesn’t always
> apply to jazz melodies, but the example below demonstrates how well it can
> work.

**Significance (p. 76).** Combines 6th-skip variation with classical counterpoint principle.

## Embellishing arpeggios with scale tones

> Embellishing an arpeggio by adding scale notes in between can be a good
> way to craft a melody that still has the chord tones on the beat but doesn’t
> sound too predictable. The line on the Cm7 chord in this example is
> constructed in this manner using a Cm9 arpeggio. A D note is added in the
> lower octave between the C and Eb.

**Significance (p. 76).** Compositional technique of interpolating scale tones in arpeggios.

## B and Ab triads — b9 #9 #11 colour

> On the F7 chord, the line combines B and Ab major triads. Both are in first
> inversion and together they add a lot of colour with the b9, #9 and #11.

**Significance (p. 77).** Names extensions produced by B/Ab triad pair over F7.

## Ebmaj7 arpeggio inversion with skip — Benson/Green

> In Example 5r the line over Cm7 has an Ebmaj7 arpeggio that begins
> on the root, but skips down to the G, then ascends the arpeggio. The
> remainder of the bar is a straight Cm7 arpeggio. This type of phrase is very
> common with George Benson and Grant Green. In fact, Grant Greens solo on
> I’ll Remember April is almost entirely built on this phrase right from the
> pick-up.

**Significance (p. 77).** Idiomatic inversion-skip pattern attributed to canonical guitarists.

## Quintal arpeggios defined

> The quintal arpeggio is also a great open sounding structure to apply to a
> Cm7 chord. Here it is used from the Eb and spells out a Cm11 sound. The
> quintal arpeggio is preceded by a diatonic enclosure of the Eb.
>
> Quintal Arpeggios (stacks of 5ths) are becoming very common melodic ideas
> in jazz. You will hear Jonathan Kreisberg and Kurt Rosenwinkel use them
> often in their solos and I will return to this topic later in this series.

**Significance (p. 78).** Defines quintal arpeggios (stacks of 5ths) with usage context and named exemplars.

## Solo study methodology

> By all means learn the full solo, but a great exercise is to break it down and
> pick out the phrases that appeal to you. After the transcription of the solo I
> have included a bar-by-bar analysis of how each line is constructed, but see if
> you can work out for yourself what’s going on before turning to the answers!
> The solo is played like a solo (i.e. it’s not exercises strung together), so in my
> explanation of the lines I focus on pointing out the concepts covered in this
> book. There is usually more than one way to think about a phrase played over
> a chord, so see if you come up with a different interpretation to me.

**Significance (p. 79).** Pedagogical framework for solo study.

## Bar 4 — harmonic minor pull

> Bar 4 — Uses the harmonic minor on Bb7 to pull towards the Eb7 (in this case
> playing the pattern of a Ddim arpeggio).

**Significance (p. 82).** Functional explanation of harmonic minor usage as dominant pull.

## Bars 5-6 — repeated statement with alteration

> Bars 5-6 — This is a very common melodic trick which makes a statement
> over the Eb7 chord and repeats it over the Edim chord, altering it slightly to
> fit. The F note in bar 5 is changed to an E in bar 6.

**Significance (p. 82).** Names a repeat-with-alteration melodic trick.

## Bar 10 — dual analysis F7 altered line

> Bar 10 — The F7 altered line can be analysed in two ways: an Ebm7b5
> arpeggio over the whole bar, or a Gbm triad for the first half of the bar and an
> Ebm7b5 arpeggio inversion for the second half.

**Significance (p. 82).** Demonstrates multiple-valid-readings principle.

## Chapter Assignments — triad pair practice

> Write a set of five or more licks over a progression, or a short solo, using:
>
> Ab and D major triad pairs
> Ab and B major triad pairs
>
> Try experimenting with using open-voiced or “spread” versions of the
> triads
>
> Stack triads to create a bigger structure, superimposing them over each
> other
>
> Spend time practising free improvisation with the triads to understand
> them better and to help connect them on the fretboard. This exercise
> will prove useful for other scale sounds too.

**Significance (p. 79).** Chapter's prescribed practice assignments.
